skill.name: linux-troubleshooting
skill.description: Linux system troubleshooting workflow for diagnosing and resolving system issues, performance problems, and service failures.
skill.summary: Guided Linux troubleshooting workflow for system, service, resource, log, and network diagnostics.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static backtick findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and fenced command examples, not Ruby execution. The skill is a legitimate Linux troubleshooting workflow, but it recommends host diagnostics, log reads, network probes, and a service restart, so it should be published with an operational safety warning.

medium_findings.title: Operational Linux Commands Require User Authorization
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends diagnostic shell commands that expose host details, processes, logs, and network state. This is appropriate for Linux troubleshooting, but it should run only on systems where the user has authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands are visible in Markdown command blocks and are normal administrative diagnostics. The risk is operational exposure, not evidence of malicious execution.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing Service Restart Guidance
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes a service restart command. Restarting a service can interrupt production traffic or change system state if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command block directly includes a restart action. The context is legitimate troubleshooting, but the operational impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution. The reviewed file contains inline skill names and fenced command examples, with no Ruby code or automatic execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown syntax and shell command examples, not Ruby source code. No code path executes these strings automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported weak cryptography on the description line, but the line contains only metadata text about Linux troubleshooting. No evidence of a cryptographic algorithm was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a plain description string. It does not reference MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive System and Log Inspection Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The command examples include system identity, kernel messages, process inspection, and log reads. These commands may reveal sensitive host or application data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present and commonly expose host, process, and log data. The file does not show exfiltration or automatic execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Diagnostic Probe
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes a curl command to a target URL and DNS lookup guidance. These are legitimate diagnostics but can contact external systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network commands are visible in the workflow. The target is a placeholder, so this is a controlled diagnostic pattern rather than confirmed exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Linux incidents often require fast checks across services, logs, resources, and networks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for safe triage and resolution planning.
actual_capabilities: Guides initial Linux system assessment with uptime, host, release, and kernel message checks.
Organizes CPU, memory, disk, I/O, and network resource analysis.
Provides process investigation steps using process lists, trees, open files, and tracing.
Supports log review through journal and system log inspection prompts.
Covers service status, service logs, restart verification, and dependency checks.
Includes resolution, monitoring, documentation, and prevention checklist steps.
limitations: It is a workflow guide and does not execute commands by itself.
Some commands require elevated privileges or installed tools such as iostat, dig, or strace.
It does not replace production change control or incident approval processes.
It uses placeholders such as service, PID, target, and domain that users must replace carefully.

use_cases.title: Triage a Slow Linux Host
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to collect resource, process, disk, and I/O evidence before selecting a fix.

use_cases.title: Investigate a Failed Service
use_cases.description: Check status, service logs, dependencies, and recent errors before restarting or changing configuration.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Incident Summary
use_cases.description: Follow the checklist to document symptoms, root cause, fix verification, monitoring, and prevention steps.

prompt_templates.title: Start Basic Triage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Linux troubleshooting workflow to help me triage this host. Ask for symptoms, recent changes, and safe read-only commands first.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Resource Pressure
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through CPU, memory, disk, I/O, and process checks for a Linux server with high load.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a Service Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the service troubleshooting phase for a failing Linux service. Separate read-only checks from any restart or state-changing step.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Incident Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Linux incident runbook from this workflow for performance, service, network, log, resolution, and prevention phases.

output_examples.input: A Linux server is slow and users report timeouts.
output: Collect uptime, recent changes, and current symptoms.
Check CPU, memory, disk, and I/O pressure with read-only commands.
Identify top processes and correlate errors from recent logs.
Recommend a fix only after evidence confirms the root cause.

output_examples.input: A web service stopped responding after deployment.
output: Review service status and service-specific logs.
Check system logs for dependency, permission, or configuration errors.
Verify network listeners and DNS only for the affected service.
Treat restart as a controlled change that needs approval.

output_examples.input: The team needs a post-incident summary.
output: Summarize symptoms, timeline, evidence gathered, and root cause.
Document the validated fix and monitoring checks.
List prevention actions and owners for follow-up work.

best_practices: Run read-only diagnostic commands before commands that change system state.
Replace placeholders with exact service names, process IDs, hosts, and domains.
Capture evidence, commands, and timestamps for incident review.
anti_patterns: Do not run restart commands on production systems without approval.
Do not paste sensitive logs, secrets, or customer data into shared tools.
Do not scan or probe networks that you are not authorized to test.

faq.question: Does this skill run Linux commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a troubleshooting workflow and command examples that a user must review and run.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can support production triage, but state-changing commands need approval and change control.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What systems does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Linux servers, services, processes, logs, resources, and network diagnostics.

faq.question: Does it include network troubleshooting?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes interface, connection, HTTP, and DNS diagnostic steps.

faq.question: What should users check before running commands?
faq.answer: Users should confirm authorization, replace placeholders, avoid sensitive data exposure, and review operational impact.
